attacks.attack: The core 5,000-9,000 PIPs/year figure is two independent guesses stacked on top of each other, and the plan's own 'combined reconciliation' quietly inflates the range used for revenue math.
attacks.evidence: Method A divides 36,000 hotels by a '~7-year renovation-trigger interval... most frequently cited' with no single source given for that interval (business-plan.md §5), producing a 3,600-6,000 spread that varies 67% depending on cycle-length assumption alone. Method B admits its multiplier (5-8x the ≥$10M LWHA transaction count) is 'unsourced... reasoning from general CRE deal-size distribution logic, not a cited study' (§5). The plan then combines these into '5,000-9,000... 6,000-7,000 midpoint' — but the very next section's revenue table silently uses 'the reconciled ~8,000-10,000 events/year planning range' (§5, revenue scenarios), a number that appears nowhere else in the document and is higher than the stated 5,000-9,000 ceiling. Every downstream revenue figure ($32K-$200K) inherits both the compounding uncertainty and this unexplained upward drift.
attacks.proposed_fix: Recompute the revenue table using the actually-stated 5,000-9,000 range (not the unexplained 8,000-10,000 figure), and publish sensitivity bands (e.g., 3,600 low / 9,000 high) rather than a single midpoint so the founder isn't anchored to a number that could be off by 2-3x.

attacks.attack: Zero first-person willingness-to-pay evidence exists for a $399 impulse-adjacent purchase, and the plan's own mitigation (owner interviews) is explicitly unrun, meaning the entire pricing and revenue section is a hypothesis dressed as a plan.
attacks.evidence: Business-plan.md §10 Risk 1 states plainly: 'Nobody in this project's research — no hotel owner, anywhere — has said "I would pay $X for this."' The $399 price is justified purely by structural analogs (ESA/PCA/reserve-study pricing shapes) and consultant-fee anchoring (§7.1), not by any observed buyer behavior. The demand-validation protocol (10-15 structured owner conversations, Van Westendorp script) is listed as 'step one of the launch plan' — i.e., not done (§10, §11). This means the single most load-bearing number in the business (price × volume = revenue) has literally zero empirical grounding, only an appeal to how unrelated categories (environmental assessments, HOA reserve studies) happen to price.
attacks.proposed_fix: Treat the plan's own 50%-threshold validation gate as a hard go/no-go: do not spend further build or GTM budget until 10-15 owner conversations are actually run and the pre-specified threshold (≥50% naming a figure in $99-$499 as a no-brainer) is met or missed.

attacks.attack: The tournament's own framing of PIPGURU/PIPGENIUS as 'validated demand, uncaptured niche' is asserted as a ruling but the underlying evidence points at least as strongly toward weak or absent demand — the plan concedes this ambiguity but still builds the whole business on the favorable reading.
attacks.evidence: Tournament-results.md Round 2 says outright: 'The niche is validated in concept but unoccupied in practice' — yet the same evidence shows both competitors are pre-revenue, waitlist-gated, have no named founder, no funding, no reviews, no Wayback Machine snapshots ever ('archived_snapshots: {}' from archive.org's own API), and near-identical copy 'suggests one operator built both as test variants' (business-plan.md §6.1-6.2, tournament-results.md Round 2). That last fact is actually evidence against an active market: if one operator ran two near-identical landing-page tests and neither converted enough to go live or iterate independently, that reads as a demand-side failure signal, not two independent entrants proving a market. Business-plan.md §10 Risk 3 itself admits this is 'an interpretation, not a proven fact' — but Section 5's revenue table and Section 8's GTM plan proceed as if the favorable interpretation is settled.
attacks.proposed_fix: Weight the go/no-go decision toward the less favorable reading until PIPBench has its own outreach data; specifically, treat the SEO/content GTM spend as a hypothesis test (does anyone actually convert from that 'winnable' query cluster) rather than a validated channel.

attacks.attack: The CMBS-distress 'why now' thesis is a poor purchase trigger for a $399 add-on cost, because the same distress that creates PIP urgency is also a classic reason owners defer discretionary spending — the plan never reconciles this internal tension.
attacks.evidence: Business-plan.md §2 describes the target owner as carrying '15-20% loan distress,' facing refinancing into a 6-8% environment, and squeezed by tariff-driven FF&E cost inflation on top of the PIP itself (§2, citing Matthews). This is precisely the profile of a buyer who is cash-constrained and deferring non-mandatory spend — yet the plan assumes this owner will confidently spend $399 (plus, per §3.1, self-serve credit-card purchase) at the exact moment they're least liquid. The plan itself frames the $399 tool as 'a real capital event... the largest capital event of their loan term' being defended by a comparatively trivial $399 purchase (§3.1) — but distressed borrowers more often cut every possible line item, including diligence tools, and lean on a broker or lender relationship they already have rather than buy a new unfamiliar SaaS product.
attacks.proposed_fix: Reframe the CMBS-distress angle as urgency for the negotiation memo's dollar-savings potential specifically (e.g., 'this $399 spend could save $50K on your PIP'), and validate in owner interviews whether distress increases or decreases willingness to spend on diligence tools — don't assume it's additive.

attacks.attack: The buyer-behavior model assumes an owner will personally research, upload a PIP document, and interpret a benchmarking report — but the plan's own evidence suggests owners default to calling a GC, broker, or the brand rep they already have a relationship with, and the product has no answer for that default path.
attacks.evidence: Business-plan.md §3.1 admits the self-serve credit-card-purchase assumption is 'an inference from observed competitor pricing structure, not a directly sourced claim about this ICP's buying behavior.' Section 8's own GTM plan leans heavily on broker referrals as 'the on-ramp' (§8 item 4) precisely because brokers 'already fight PIP surprises mid-deal' — which is itself evidence that the deal-side relationship (broker, GC, lender) is the existing behavioral default, not a self-directed owner researching benchmarks online. Section 6.3 further shows the real, paid comp (Beyer Brown) operates as a human consulting relationship with named enterprise clients (Davidson Hospitality, Hudson Advisors, Wheelock Street) — i.e., real money in this category currently flows through relationship-based engagement, not self-serve software, and PIPBench has no evidence that a single-owner-operator will break that pattern to use a tool instead of calling someone they already trust.
attacks.proposed_fix: Test the actual purchase path directly in the owner interviews: ask not 'would you pay $399' but 'what would you do first when you get a PIP' — if the honest answer is consistently 'call my GC/broker,' the product needs a broker-embedded distribution model as v1, not phase-2.

attacks.attack: The 1%/5% capture-rate revenue scenarios use capture rate against total U.S. PIP volume, but PIPBench's real addressable funnel is far narrower — SEO-driven, self-serve, English-language, credit-card-comfortable owners who find the site and choose to trust an unbranded product's benchmark over their broker/GC/consultant — making even the 1% floor likely optimistic.
attacks.evidence: Business-plan.md §5 computes '1% capture → ~80-100 reports/year → ~$32K-$40K' directly off the 8,000-10,000 total-PIP planning range, implicitly treating 1% as a low, conservative floor. But the GTM plan (§8) is SEO- and PR-first with no paid acquisition budget specified, competing for a 'winnable' but currently low-authority query cluster against consulting-firm content marketers and CMBS lending-content sites already ranking (§8 item 1). No traffic, conversion-rate, or funnel-size estimate is given anywhere in the plan to justify that 1% of all U.S. PIPs nationally will even discover PIPBench, let alone purchase — the capture-rate table treats 'percent of total market' as if it maps directly onto 'percent of a marketing funnel,' skipping the awareness and trust-building step entirely for a brand-new, unbranded, no-social-proof product (as documented for the two direct competitors' own zero-traction histories in §6.1-6.2).
attacks.proposed_fix: Replace the capture-rate table with a funnel model (organic traffic estimate for the target query cluster → conversion rate → purchase rate) benchmarked against comparable SEO-driven diligence-tool businesses, rather than a bare percentage of the total addressable PIP count.

attacks.attack: The $399 price has zero first-person evidentiary basis — it is pure structural analogy (ESA/PCA/HOA reserve-study pricing shapes) and consultant-fee anchoring, and the business-plan itself admits nobody has been asked.
attacks.evidence: business-plan.md §10 Risk 1: 'Nobody — not one hotel owner — has told this project's research they would pay $399 for this... The $5,000–$9,000 annual PIP-count used throughout this plan is a derived planning estimate, not a citable industry statistic.' launch-plan.md Phase 0 treats the demand-validation protocol as still-to-be-run, and its own success threshold requires only 'at least 7 of 10-15' owners naming $99-$499 as a no-brainer — a bar so low (roughly half of a tiny, self-selected, non-random sample) that it would not survive scrutiny from any real investor or lender, and even that hasn't been attempted.
attacks.proposed_fix: Do not treat $399 as fixed. Run the Phase 0 protocol with a larger, less self-selected sample (25-30 conversations, not 10-15) before any content/PR spend, and be willing to publish a price range rather than a single anchor until conversations produce convergent numbers.

attacks.attack: The revenue ceiling is structurally tiny: the plan's own math tops out at ~$200K/yr at a 5% capture rate of an already-unsourced 5,000-9,000 PIPs/year estimate, meaning even a wildly optimistic best case is sub-$250K/yr solo income with no clear path past it inside Architecture A.
attacks.evidence: business-plan.md §5 table: 5% capture → 'approx $160,000-$200,000/year' revenue; 1% capture → '$32,000-$40,000/year.' The plan calls this 'a real-but-modest business at low capture, not a venture-scale projection' (§5, citing offer-pricing.md §2-3) — i.e., the founder's own research concedes the ceiling. Capture rates above single digits are implausible against two live pre-revenue competitors already contesting the identical query cluster and Beyer Brown occupying the high end of the same demand.
attacks.proposed_fix: Either accept this explicitly as a lifestyle/side business (not a scalable company) and size effort accordingly, or make Architecture B (broker/lender seat licenses) the real bet from day one rather than a phase-2 afterthought — but see the next finding on how unvalidated that assumption is.

attacks.attack: Architecture B (broker/lender seat licenses, the only line item that could meaningfully move total revenue beyond ~$200K/yr) rests on zero willingness-to-pay evidence of any kind — not even the thin structural-analogy reasoning used for Architecture A.
attacks.evidence: business-plan.md §7.3: 'this rests entirely on unverified broker/lender willingness-to-pay assumptions with no evidence base yet collected.' launch-plan.md Architecture B section repeats verbatim that the $60,000-$120,000/year estimate 'depends entirely on unverified broker/lender adoption assumptions with no evidence base yet (no broker/lender WTP research exists in this project's files as of this writing).' Section 3.2 of business-plan.md also flags the lender-as-customer idea itself as pure 'inference from escrow mechanics, not observed demand.' No broker or lender interview, survey, or comparable-pricing research exists anywhere in the cited files.
attacks.proposed_fix: Before counting on Architecture B in any model, run the same conversation-based validation protocol used for owners against 10-15 brokers/lenders specifically on seat-license pricing and usage frequency — currently this revenue line is invented, not researched, and should be removed from any pitch deck or projection until it has its own evidence base.

attacks.attack: The plan has no support/ops cost or time model for a solo founder handling a $399 memo that an owner will hand to a franchisor or GC in a live negotiation — disputes over an 'over-range' flag that costs the owner credibility with their brand rep, or a benchmark that turns out wrong for their specific market, are inevitable and unbudgeted.
attacks.evidence: business-plan.md §7.2 describes the report as 'a database lookup, an escalation calculation, and a templated memo generation, not a human analyst's time' — explicitly modeling zero marginal labor per report. But §4.2 concedes the dataset is uneven (luxury/economy/extended-stay lean 'Directional,' i.e., interpolated) and §10 Risk 4 flags this openly. Nowhere in business-plan.md or launch-plan.md is there a support workflow, a refund policy, an SLA, or any founder-hours estimate for handling a customer who paid $399, used the memo in a real negotiation with their brand, and got push-back or a worse outcome. The budget table in launch-plan.md (lines 183-202) itemizes domain, LLC, ToS review, SEO, and events but has no support/labor line at all.
attacks.proposed_fix: Add an explicit support-cost and time-budget line (even a placeholder), plus a written refund/dispute policy addressing the specific case of a memo that a brand or GC contests or rejects, before taking payment from a single customer.

attacks.attack: No cost line anywhere in the plan covers data maintenance (keeping the Nehmer/HVS-derived benchmark dataset current as tariffs, cost indices, and brand FDDs change year to year) or legal/E&O exposure from a negotiation product that owners will use as ammunition in real dollar disputes with franchisors.
attacks.evidence: launch-plan.md's budget table (lines 187-202) lists domain, entity formation, TESS search, ToS review, hosting, content, PR, community, events, and broker pilot — but has no line for annual dataset refresh, escalation-index updates, or professional/E&O liability insurance, despite the product explicitly generating benchmark 'flags' and a 'negotiation memo' meant to be used against a franchisor's stated cost claims (business-plan.md §4.1). The only legal-adjacent budget item is a one-time '$0-$500+ [estimated]' ToS/disclaimer review (launch-plan.md line 192), which is a document review, not an insurance policy or an ongoing legal-review retainer.
attacks.proposed_fix: Add explicit recurring line items: (1) annual data-refresh time/cost to keep Turner Building Cost Index, RLB factors, tariff data, and FDD-derived figures current, and (2) obtain a real E&O/professional-liability insurance quote before launch, sized to the actual product (a paid deliverable used in real estate negotiations), not skip it because the disclaimer says 'not legal advice.'

attacks.attack: The plan assumes SEO and trade-press PR will convert into paying customers at the volumes needed to hit even the 1% capture case, but the only competitive evidence cited is that the query cluster is 'winnable' — there is no evidence PIPBench's content will actually outrank or out-convert two existing (if vaporware) competitors who already occupy that exact query space with live pages.
attacks.evidence: business-plan.md §8 point 1 and launch-plan.md's SEO section both assert the 'hotel PIP cost' cluster is winnable because 'no authoritative, purpose-built self-serve benchmarking tool currently owns' it — but PIPGURU is confirmed to already be a 'live site' ranking in that space (business-plan.md §6.1), meaning PIPBench's 10 planned articles must outrank an incumbent, not enter an empty field. No SEO difficulty metrics, keyword-volume data, or backlink analysis is cited anywhere in icp-market-channels.md §3.6 beyond a qualitative characterization of current SERP composition. The whole GTM funnel (SEO + PR + community) has no historical conversion-rate benchmark attached — the plan supplies zero data on how many organic visitors it takes to produce one $399 sale.
attacks.proposed_fix: Treat the SEO/PR channel as unproven until Phase 1 produces real traffic-to-paid-conversion data (as the plan's own Gate 2 acknowledges), and set a hard time/spend cap on content production before revisiting the channel mix rather than assuming 10 articles will suffice.

attacks.attack: The demand-validation protocol's own 'go' bar is weak enough that a founder motivated to proceed could pass it on a handful of enthusiastic-but-unrepresentative conversations, undermining the plan's claim that validation gates real investment.
attacks.evidence: launch-plan.md Gate 1 requires only 'at least 3 of 15 conversations produce a genuine yes' as the kill-criteria floor, and 'at least 7 of 10-15' naming a price as acceptable for a go signal — thresholds explicitly self-set by the founder with no external benchmark for what a legitimate go/no-go bar should be for a B2B service at this price point (offer-pricing.md §4, reproduced in launch-plan.md, flags thresholds as '[recommend founder set these explicitly before launch, not after seeing results]' but does not cite any comparable-product validation-rate benchmark).
attacks.proposed_fix: Benchmark the go/no-go thresholds against known early-stage B2B validation practice (e.g., require the sample to include owners outside the founder's existing network) rather than self-set numbers with no external anchor.

attacks.attack: The $399 Full Benchmark price is justified largely by discount-to-consultant framing ($5,000-$15,000 anchor), but that anchor itself is partly a dated, non-comparable figure — the plan's own Section 2 flags a $7,500-$20,000 franchisor PIP-document-preparation fee from a 2017 CoStar panel as 'a separate and dated figure not to be confused with renovation-cost estimating,' raising the risk that pricing-narrative anchors conflate two different services.
attacks.evidence: business-plan.md §2: 'franchisors themselves charge $7,500–$20,000 just to produce the PIP document, per a 2017 CoStar panel of Marriott and Hilton executives — a separate and dated figure not to be confused with renovation-cost estimating.' The plan is careful to label this correctly internally, but the risk is that external marketing copy (SEO articles, PR pitches) blurs this distinction under commercial pressure to make the discount-to-consultant story as dramatic as possible, especially since Section 10 Risk 7 already flags a pattern of banned-but-tempting anecdotes in this exact plan.
attacks.proposed_fix: Keep the consultant-fee anchor strictly to the $5,000-$15,000 cost-estimating figure in all external copy, and enforce (via the same claims-discipline review already mandated for banned claims) that the 2017 franchisor-fee figure never appears in marketing material even as an aside.

attacks.attack: The dataset is built on a stale 2021 (13th ed.) Nehmer/HVS guide even though the project's own competitor research found the guide has since reached an 18th edition (2026 release) — meaning a current-year version of the exact same primary source likely exists and could resolve the escalation problem directly, but the team never obtained it.
attacks.evidence: benchmarks.json sources.nehmer-2021 cites 'Nehmer & HVS Design, Hotel Cost Estimating Guide 2021 (13th ed.)' and every category benchmark is tagged basis:2021. But run-1/design/competitor-teardown.md lines 101-108 independently found: '"Now in its 18th year — the 2026 edition" was released by Jonathan Nehmer + Associates and HVS Design' and that it is obtainable free via a lead-gen form at nehmer.com/costguide. The escalation.basis_2021_to_2026 note in benchmarks.json even states '[estimated]... No direct 2021-2026 PIP escalation index exists publicly' — a claim contradicted by the project's own research showing the exact successor edition exists and was simply not filled out via the lead-gen form to get current figures.
attacks.proposed_fix: Before launch, fill out the nehmer.com/costguide lead-gen form and pull the 2026 (18th ed.) figures directly, replacing the entire 1.25-1.35 'derived/estimated' escalation band with Verified 2026 numbers from the primary source itself. If the newer edition can't be obtained in time, the marketing/UI must disclose that a newer edition of the cited primary source exists and was not used — not just that escalation is 'directional.'

attacks.attack: The 1.25-1.35x escalation multiplier applied to every 2021 figure is a two-input guess with no shown arithmetic, and it is internally inconsistent with the dataset's own 2024-to-2026 band.
attacks.evidence: benchmarks.json: basis_2021_to_2026 = {low:1.25, high:1.35}, note: '[estimated] ... PIP costs reported >30% above pre-COVID levels by 2024 (Matthews/Hunter) plus Turner ~4.9%/yr 2024-2026.' Reverse-engineering: if 2024-2026 is 1.09-1.10 (basis_2024_to_2026, the dataset's own adjacent field), then 2021-2024 implied by the total band is only ~1.14-1.24 (1.25/1.10 to 1.35/1.09) — barely reconciling with an independently-cited '>30% above pre-COVID' figure, which on its own reading implies ≥1.30 for 2021-2024 alone before any 2024-2026 escalation is even added. The two adjacent 'directional' fields in the same JSON file don't multiply out consistently, and no worked calculation is shown to check.
attacks.proposed_fix: Show the actual arithmetic (2021→2024 factor × 2024→2026 factor) in the dataset notes so the two directional bands are provably consistent with each other, or collapse to one clearly-derived number instead of two that don't reconcile.

attacks.attack: The dataset extracts specific priced figures and page citations from a copyrighted, all-rights-reserved industry guide (gated behind a lead-gen form, not an open dataset) and resells them as the core paid content of a $99-$399 commercial product, with zero mention of data licensing or fair-use analysis anywhere in the business plan.
attacks.evidence: benchmarks.json cites 'nehmer-2021' with explicit page numbers (e.g. 'Summary pp.10-11', 'Economy p.13') for the large majority of Verified figures — this is not independent re-measurement, it is transcription of the guide's own published unit-cost tables. HVS's own site states '© Copyright 2026. All Rights Reserved by HVS' (fetched directly from hvs.com). Feist v. Rural doctrine protects facts (raw dollar figures) from copyright but explicitly protects the compiler's original selection, arrangement, and presentation of a compilation — Nehmer/HVS's specific tier/category breakdown, ranges, and formatting is exactly the kind of selection-and-arrangement a compilation-copyright claim would target, and the guide itself was obtained through a lead-gen form whose terms of service (not reviewed anywhere in this project) may contractually restrict redistribution regardless of the underlying facts' copyright status. Neither business-plan.md, business-design.md, nor benchmarks.json anywhere mention having reviewed nehmer.com's or hvs.com's terms of use, or having taken any fair-use / compilation-copyright legal opinion before building a commercial resale product on this extraction.
attacks.proposed_fix: Before launch, (1) actually read the terms attached to the nehmer.com/costguide lead-gen download and hvs.com terms of use for redistribution restrictions, (2) get a lawyer's fair-use/Feist opinion specifically on transcribing a competitor's compiled figures with page citations into a resold paid product, and (3) consider re-deriving the underlying facts independently (e.g., citing the same raw cost categories but sourcing figures from multiple independent surveys) rather than page-citing a single copyrighted guide's own tables verbatim.

attacks.attack: The demo property's own tier (upper-midscale) is disproportionately built from interpolation rather than sourced figures, undermining the product's central credibility claim exactly where the flagship demo is shown.
attacks.evidence: business-plan.md §4.1 states the demo property is 'a fictional 118-key upper-midscale property.' In benchmarks.json, nearly every upper-midscale figure is grade:'directional'/source:'derived': guestroom-softgoods.upper-midscale (interpolated between Midscale/Upscale), guestroom-full-add.upper-midscale (scaled '~1.8x' from Economy), guest-bathrooms.upper-midscale and upper-midscale-full-add (both scaled from Economy 'tier ratio ~1.8x'), corridors.upper-midscale (same 1.8x scaling). The business plan itself calls out only ONE of these (softgoods) as the example of directional grading (§4.1, 'For example... Upper-Midscale figure... is interpolated'), but doesn't disclose that at least four other categories for the same tier use the identical repeated 1.8x scaling trick rather than independent sourcing — i.e., the demo's benchmark 'depth' is largely one ratio reapplied five times, not five independently verified data points.
attacks.proposed_fix: Either source at least the bathroom/corridor/full-add upper-midscale figures independently (a second public source, even directional) rather than reusing the same 1.8x heuristic repeatedly, or make the UI/marketing explicitly show 'these N figures derive from the same scaling assumption' so a buyer isn't misled into reading repeated derived numbers as independent corroboration.

attacks.attack: The dataset's granular lineItems array — the only place real per-item benchmarking exists — covers just Economy and Upper-Upscale tiers, meaning the demo property's own upper-midscale tier has zero line-item-level benchmark coverage, only category-level directional estimates.
attacks.evidence: benchmarks.json lineItems array: all 10 entries are tier:'economy' or tier:'upper-upscale' (e.g. carpet-pad-econ, carpet-pad-uu, window-treatments-econ, bedsets-econ...). No upper-midscale line item appears anywhere in that array. business-plan.md §4.2 already admits this in general ('a meaningful but partial set of granular items... at the Economy and Upper-Upscale tiers only'), but doesn't connect that admission to the fact that this is precisely the tier of the property used in every demo screenshot and walkthrough — so the tool's most granular capability is invisible on its own flagship example.
attacks.proposed_fix: Either add upper-midscale line-item entries (even 3-4 commonly cited ones, transparently sourced) before using the upper-midscale demo property as the showcase, or swap the public demo to an Economy or Upper-Upscale property where line-item benchmarking actually has coverage to display.

attacks.attack: There is no evidence the PIP scope-ingestion parser has ever been tested against a real, messy PIP document — only against one internally engineered fictional composite — so the plan's claim that owners can 'paste or upload the PIP's scope' and get it auto-categorized into the 9 Nehmer categories is unvalidated against real-world formatting variance (brand-specific line-item naming, bundled scope lines, non-Nehmer category schemes used by IHG/Hilton/Marriott PIP templates).
attacks.evidence: business-plan.md §4.1 step 2 describes 'the parser categorizes them into the nine industry-standard categories used by the Nehmer/HVS Hotel Cost Estimating Guide,' and §4.1 closing paragraph states 'the sample PIP modeled for this build is a fictional 118-key... composite.' No file in business-plan.md, business-design.md, or competitor-teardown.md cites having run the parser against an actual franchisor-issued PIP PDF or scope-of-work document (real PIPs are typically brand-formatted spreadsheets or Word/PDF documents organized by the brand's own item numbering, not Nehmer's category taxonomy). Real PIPs also routinely bundle multiple cost drivers into single line items (e.g., 'guestroom renovation package' as one lump sum) that would not map cleanly into 9 discrete categories the way an engineered demo can.
attacks.proposed_fix: Before charging $399 for the Full Benchmark, source 3-5 real (redacted/anonymized) PIP documents from public deal filings, broker case studies, or a willing pilot owner, and test the parser's category-mapping accuracy against them, reporting a real match/miss rate rather than relying solely on the engineered demo.

attacks.attack: The entire FF&E category — arguably the line item most affected by the plan's own headline tariff/cost-escalation narrative — rests on a single non-primary trade-vendor source for all three tiers, contradicting the product's 'named-source, honestly graded' differentiation claim.
attacks.evidence: benchmarks.json categories 'ffe-per-room': all three tiers (economy, midscale, luxury) are grade:'directional', source:'onestophotelffe', with the sources registry itself labeling it 'trade-vendor content, not primary advisory' — the single weakest-typed source in the entire registry alongside 'amerailsys' (also 'contractor content (directional)'). Yet business-plan.md §2 leads with FF&E tariff impact as a primary 'why now' driver ('tariffs have added an estimated 5-10%+ to FF&E costs'), meaning the category most rhetorically emphasized in the pitch is the one most thinly sourced in the actual product dataset.
attacks.proposed_fix: Either find a second, more authoritative FF&E cost source (e.g., an FF&E-procurement firm's public pricing commentary, Beyer Brown's own published ranges if any exist) to corroborate onestophotelffe's figures, or soften the FF&E-cost-escalation emphasis in marketing copy to match the actual confidence grade of the underlying data.

results.surface: Legal and brand risk: franchisor trademark nominative fair use, disclaimer/reliance liability, PIPBench trademark clearance sequencing, CAN-SPAM outreach compliance, founder employment/IP-assignment conflict silence

attacks.attack: The dataset and marketing use specific franchisor brand names and program names ("Hampton Inn (Hilton)", "Forever Young", "Holiday Inn Express (IHG)", "Formula Blue") in the actual commercial product, and site copy names "Hampton Inn" in a public-facing SEO article title, not just in an internal research citation — this is a materially larger surface than a single disclaimer sentence can cover.
attacks.evidence: product/data/benchmarks.json lines 61-108 embed brand+program names directly as product line-item labels (not just source citations), e.g. `{"brand": "Hampton Inn (Hilton)", "program": "Forever Young", ...}`. launch-plan.md line 82 proposes publishing an SEO article literally titled "Hampton Inn PIP Cost Guide: What Upper-Midscale Renovations Actually Run, By Category." site/index.html line 595 only has one generic disclaimer line ("Brand names appear only to identify publicly documented renovation programs... not affiliated with, endorsed by, or sponsored by any hotel franchisor") covering the whole site, but nominative fair use requires the use be no more than reasonably necessary to identify the goods/services and not suggest sponsorship — using the branded program names ("Forever Young", "Formula Blue") as marketing hooks in article titles goes beyond identifying-the-brand-for-comparison into using the brand's own proprietary program name as a traffic-driving keyword, which is a materially riskier use than citing "Hampton Inn" itself.
attacks.proposed_fix: Keep brand/program names in the internal dataset for accuracy, but scrub franchisor-specific program names (Forever Young, Formula Blue) from public marketing surfaces and SEO titles; replace "Hampton Inn PIP Cost Guide" with a generic "Upper-Midscale Brand PIP Cost Guide" framing, reserving specific brand mentions for factual, minimally-necessary references (e.g., a single sentence citing the source), and have trademark counsel review the final SEO article list before publication, not just the disclaimer footer.

attacks.attack: The plan's entire liability shield is a single UI banner and one FAQ sentence ("negotiation support, not legal or engineering advice") with no underlying ToS, liability waiver, or arbitration clause drafted or even scoped as a launch-blocking deliverable — the launch plan treats ToS review as a parallel checklist item, not a precondition for taking payment.
attacks.evidence: launch-plan.md Step 2 says only: "have a lawyer (or, at minimum, a qualified legal-template review) confirm the site's disclaimer language matches the binding liability posture" — it does not require an actual Terms of Service, limitation-of-liability clause, or indemnification agreement to exist before the Week 2 landing page goes live or before Week 4+ paid checkout begins. business-plan.md §10 risk 6 states the disclaimer removes legal risk ("This does not create legal risk to PIPBench's own product — the memo is explicitly labeled negotiation support, not a legal opinion") — this is an unsupported legal conclusion by the business plan's own authors, not an actual attorney's opinion. A banner disclaimer does not bind a customer contractually; only an accepted ToS/EULA with a liability cap, no-warranty clause, and governing-law provision does, and the plan has no line item requiring that document to exist before checkout goes live.
attacks.proposed_fix: Make 'ToS with liability cap, no-warranty clause, and clickwrap acceptance at checkout' an explicit Phase 0 gate blocking any 'Get my benchmark' checkout button from going live (not just a parallel Week 1-3 checklist item), and get an actual attorney opinion — not the business plan's own reasoning — on whether the banner+FAQ language is sufficient before a single paid report is sold.

attacks.attack: A hotel owner could rely on a PIPBench benchmark or negotiation memo to reject a franchisor's PIP number, lose the negotiation or damage the franchise relationship (risking termination per the plan's own problem statement), and sue PIPBench for the resulting loss — the plan has no professional liability (E&O) insurance line anywhere in its budget, and 'Directional' (estimated/interpolated) grades are precisely the numbers most likely to be wrong in a way that causes real financial harm.
attacks.evidence: business-plan.md §2 states non-compliance with a PIP "risks termination of the franchise itself, which for a small owner can mean losing the business." business-plan.md §4.1 confirms non-midscale tiers "generally show more Directional badges" (i.e., weaker, interpolated data) — meaning the owners most likely to get a shaky number are exactly the ones for whom a bad negotiation is highest-stakes. The launch-plan.md budget table (lines 187-202) itemizes domain, LLC formation, trademark search, ToS review — but contains no E&O/professional liability or general liability insurance line at all, despite the product's output being explicitly designed to be handed to a franchisor or GC as negotiation ammunition (business-plan.md §4.1, step 5).
attacks.proposed_fix: Add a professional liability (errors & omissions) insurance line to the Phase 0 budget as a launch precondition, not an afterthought, and have the ToS explicitly disclaim reliance on Directional-graded figures with a clear per-figure confidence flag the memo restates in its own delivered output (not only in the in-app UI), since the memo itself — not the UI — is the document an owner will actually hand to a franchisor.

attacks.attack: PIPBench trademark clearance was never actually done — only a plain web search — and the plan schedules the real USPTO TESS search for Week 1 of launch, in the same week as domain registration and landing-page drafting, meaning real marketing spend and public use of the mark could precede clearance and create rebranding/refund exposure if a conflict surfaces.
attacks.evidence: brand/naming-and-domain.md line 28 states plainly: "a proper USPTO/trademark counsel search is a launch-plan step; not performed in this run" and that the only check done was "a web search for 'PIPBench'/'pip bench' software" returning "only unrelated Python packaging tools." launch-plan.md Step 2 and the 90-Day Calendar schedule the actual TESS search for the same week as domain registration and landing-page drafting — the calendar (line 140-142) shows "Landing page live" in Week 2, before Week 3's "complete... trademark search," meaning public use under the name can begin before clearance is confirmed.
attacks.proposed_fix: Sequence the plan so the TESS knockout search (and ideally a common-law/state-registration search) completes and clears before domain registration or any public-facing landing page goes live under the PIPBench name, not in the same week as or after those steps — a name search is cheap and fast; a forced rebrand after real traffic and PR outreach is not.

attacks.attack: The launch plan's PR and community-outreach tactics have no compliance framework for the mechanics of mass outreach — pitching 8 named trade-press outlets and designing a waitlist/email-capture landing page is treated purely as a content-quality question (don't post spam, follow community rules) with zero mention of CAN-SPAM-style requirements (identifiable sender, physical address, opt-out mechanism) for any resulting email list.
attacks.evidence: launch-plan.md's Trade-press pitch plan (lines 94-102) names eight outlets to pitch directly with no process for how pitches are sent (cold email implied) or how any resulting owner email list (from the "Get my benchmark"/waitlist landing-page mechanism referenced at launch-plan.md line 21) is later used for follow-up marketing. The Community presence rules (lines 104-108) address only self-promotion etiquette, not commercial-email requirements. No section of the plan mentions CAN-SPAM's required elements (accurate header/from info, physical postal address, functioning opt-out) for follow-up emails that would naturally flow from the waitlist-capture mechanism the plan itself designs.
attacks.proposed_fix: Add a short compliance note to Phase 1 requiring any commercial email sent to landing-page waitlist signups or trade-press contacts to include a valid physical address and working unsubscribe mechanism per CAN-SPAM, and to honor opt-outs promptly — a cheap, mechanical fix given the plan already designs the waitlist-capture flow that will generate this obligation.

attacks.attack: The plan is entirely silent on the category of risk that the founder's own outreach, content-writing, and work-hours time investment in building/marketing a for-profit commercial tool may implicate an existing employer's IP-assignment or moonlighting policy — a real and common category of risk for a side-project founder that the plan's otherwise-thorough honesty register never once names.
attacks.evidence: business-plan.md §10 (Risks & Honest Unknowns) lists 7 detailed risk items and launch-plan.md's Risk Section restates 8 — covering WTP evidence, market-sizing derivation, competitor ambiguity, dataset confidence, Procore platform risk, franchisor relations, banned claims, and AAHOA pricing — but neither document contains any item addressing whether the founder's employment agreement (invention-assignment, non-compete, or moonlighting clauses common to full-time employment contracts) could affect ownership of the PIPBench IP, the dataset, or the founder's right to build/launch it at all. This is a standard, foreseeable diligence category for any side-project founder and its complete absence from an otherwise exhaustively self-critical honesty register is itself the gap.
attacks.proposed_fix: Add a risk-register item requiring the founder to review their own current employment agreement (IP-assignment scope, moonlighting/outside-work clauses, non-compete terms) before forming the LLC or accepting payment, and to document that review's outcome — this is a category check applicable regardless of who the founder's employer is, and costs nothing but the founder's own time to perform.

attacks.attack: PIPGURU has moved past pure-landing-page vaporware since the project's own teardown was written — it now has live /auth/signup, /auth/login, /auth/forgot-password pages and a /checkout/single path, none of which existed in the 'every CTA routes to #waitlist' finding the plan relies on.
attacks.evidence: Live fetch today (2026-07-18) of thepipguru.com/auth/signup returns a working 'Create your password' account-creation form, and /dashboard 302s to a real /auth/login screen with email+password fields — infrastructure absent from `run-1/design/competitor-teardown.md` §1's finding, dated the same day, that 'every purchase CTA... routes to a waitlist form, not checkout.' The homepage itself still shows 'Buy one estimate' linking to `thepipguru.com/checkout/single` (a 404 for a specific estimate ID, not a dead route) — i.e., a checkout system exists, it just isn't populated yet. The plan's entire competitive framing ('pre-revenue, single-page marketing/waitlist site simulating a live SaaS product') is already partially stale on the day it was written, and the launch-plan's monthly tripwire cadence (`run-1/business/launch-plan.md` lines 173-179) is too slow to catch a competitor that can stand up auth/checkout infrastructure within the same news cycle.
attacks.proposed_fix: Tighten the competitor tripwire from monthly to weekly for the first 90 days, and add 'auth/checkout infrastructure appearing behind previously-decorative CTAs' as an explicit trigger condition alongside the existing 'named customer' and 'exited beta' triggers — infrastructure stand-up is a leading indicator that precedes a named customer by weeks, not a lagging one.

attacks.attack: The plan's own moat argument — 'no incumbent gatekeeper... built entirely from free/already-public sources' (business-design.md §9 / business-plan.md §9) — is also the strongest reason the primary source-holder (HVS/Nehmer) is the single cheapest, most credible actor to build a competing calculator, and nothing in the plan monitors this or treats it as anything but a passive reference product.
attacks.evidence: `run-1/business/business-plan.md` §6.4 and `run-1/design/competitor-teardown.md` §4 both confirm the Nehmer/HVS guide is PIPBench's own primary data backbone and is free, lead-gen-gated, updated annually, in its 18th year, with 5 named contributing GC firms — i.e., HVS/Nehmer already has the authoritative brand, the sourcing relationships, and a lead-gen funnel actively collecting owner contact info. Turning that static PDF into a web calculator is a materially smaller lift for them than PIPBench's own build was, and they inherit instant credibility PIPBench has to earn from zero. Neither `business-plan.md` §10 nor the launch-plan's competitor-tripwire routine (`run-1/business/launch-plan.md` lines 173-179) names HVS/Nehmer as a monitored entity at all — only thepipguru.com and thepipgenius.com are on the watch list.
attacks.proposed_fix: Add nehmer.com/costguide and hvs.com to the monthly tripwire monitoring list specifically for 'has an interactive calculator or web tool appeared alongside the static guide,' and treat any such move as an immediate-reassessment trigger, not a monthly-cadence one — HVS launching a free calculator would eliminate PIPBench's price advantage overnight since HVS pays nothing for the underlying data it already owns.

attacks.attack: Owners can already get a rough version of this exact deliverable for free by pasting their PIP into a frontier AI chatbot and asking it to benchmark line items and draft pushback language — a substitute product with zero marginal cost to the owner that no file in this project's research addresses or prices in anywhere.
attacks.evidence: The product's own stated mechanism is 'a database lookup, an escalation calculation, and a templated memo generation, not a human analyst's time' (`run-1/business/business-plan.md` §7.2) — a workflow well within a current frontier model's reach given the same public sources (Nehmer/HVS figures, FDD filings, cost indices) PIPBench cites are equally accessible to any owner who asks a chatbot to search and reason over them. Nothing in `business-plan.md` §10 (Risks & Honest Unknowns), `business-design.md` §7 (honesty register), or `competitor-teardown.md` §5 (adjacent threats) mentions general-purpose AI assistants as a competitive or substitute force at all — the honesty register names Procore and franchisors as adjacent threats but is silent on the free option every target owner already has a browser tab open to. This is the single largest unaddressed gap in an otherwise unusually thorough risk register.
attacks.proposed_fix: Add an explicit honesty-register entry acknowledging the free-chatbot substitute, and sharpen the differentiation pitch specifically against it: the negotiation memo's category-standardized structure (Nehmer/HVS's nine categories), the per-line Verified/Directional confidence grading tied to named sources, and the saved/refreshable property record are all things a one-off chatbot conversation does not reliably reproduce or persist — but this argument needs to be made explicitly in marketing copy, because right now the plan has not even conceded the threat exists, let alone rehearsed the answer to a prospect who says 'I just asked Claude.'

attacks.attack: PIPGENIUS has already missed its own publicly stated launch deadline — its site still reads 'onboarding the first wave of founding clients in May 2026' in future tense on July 18, 2026 — a signal of stall that the plan's own tripwire logic doesn't capture because it looks for a positive launch, not a quietly blown deadline.
attacks.evidence: Direct fetch of thepipgenius.com today (2026-07-18) shows the hero and footer both still stating 'Onboarding the first wave of founding clients in May 2026' and 'Founding Clients · Private Beta' — unchanged, future-tense language nearly three months past the date it names. `run-1/design/competitor-teardown.md` §2 documented this same May 2026 claim as of the teardown's own write date; the plan's tripwire (`run-1/business/launch-plan.md` lines 173-179) is built to fire on 'exited private beta' or a named customer appearing, not on 'said a date and quietly let it pass,' so this ambiguous signal (stalled build vs. simply stale marketing copy while real onboarding proceeds privately) would not currently trigger any reassessment either way.
attacks.proposed_fix: Add 'stated launch/onboarding date passed with no public status change' as its own tripwire condition, distinct from 'exited beta' — a blown deadline is weak evidence of demand weakness (supporting the plan's own 'ambiguous stall' framing in Risk 3) and should be logged, not ignored, at the next monthly check.

attacks.attack: Procore's own 2026 public messaging already frames owner-facing, portfolio-level AI cost forecasting as a near-term product direction, which is a more direct signal toward owner-side PIP benchmarking than the plan's 'monitor, don't treat as blocking' framing (based on the May 2026 contract-review launch alone) currently accounts for.
attacks.evidence: A live search today surfaced Procore's own blog post title '5 Construction Tech Trends in 2026 That Will Drive True [Predictability]' with the stated claim 'Owners will use AI models trained on their entire historical portfolio to forecast cash flow and total project cost with 95% accuracy' (procore.com/library/voices/2026-construction-tech-trends) — first-party Procore language naming owners, not just GCs, as the intended AI-forecasting user. `run-1/design/business-design.md` §7.5 and `business-plan.md` §10 Risk 5 base the 'non-blocking, GC-facing only' conclusion solely on the May 2026 AI contract-review launch and explicitly state 'no evidence found of Procore building hotel-brand-specific PIP logic' — true, but the owner-facing framing in Procore's own newer content wasn't in scope for that finding and shifts the risk slightly.
attacks.proposed_fix: Add Procore's public content/roadmap messaging (blog, investor materials, product pages) to the monitoring routine alongside its product pages, since Procore signals its direction in marketing content before shipping features — a purely product-page check will lag the signal by months.

results.surface: Execution and the artifact itself — gaps between site/plan promises and what the demo and launch plan actually deliver

attacks.attack: The site's core differentiator claim — "Real document ingestion... an actual PIP-style scope table being parsed" — rests on a brittle regex that only works on the exact dot-leader format of the hardcoded sample text, not on any real-world PIP document format.
attacks.evidence: product/app.js line 133: `const lineRe = /^([\d.]+)\s+(.+?)\s*\.{3,}\s*\$?([\d,]+)\s*$/;` requires each line to match `ref  description ....... $amount` with 3+ literal dots as a separator. product/data/sample-pip.json's rawText is hand-formatted to satisfy exactly this pattern (`............ $1,552,000`). Real PIP scope tables arrive as PDF/Excel/Word tables; copy-pasting one into the textarea will not reproduce dot-leader formatting, so the parser will silently fail to match nearly every line. site/index.html step 02 promises "Upload or paste the scope table. PIPBench parses and sorts it" as if this generalizes — it doesn't, and there is no PDF/file upload at all (no `<input type=file>` or upload code anywhere in product/index.html or app.js).
attacks.proposed_fix: Either build a real parser tolerant of tab/space/multi-column layouts (and ideally actual PDF/table extraction) before claiming "real document ingestion," or change the site copy to say the demo works on a specific pasted-text format and that production document ingestion (PDF/Excel) is not yet built.

attacks.attack: The product's Step 1 "property intake" is not actually an input step — it's a read-only display of one hardcoded fictional property — so the site's promise of an interactive four-step flow is only true for steps 2-4, not step 1.
attacks.evidence: site/index.html step 01 copy: "Brand, flag family, keys, chain scale, state, and what triggered the PIP." But product/index.html panel-1 only renders `renderIntake()` from the fixed SAMPLE.property object (app.js lines 96-106) with no form fields; the only button is "Continue." The demo itself discloses this honestly via the `env-chip` ("DEMO · fictional property"), but the marketing page's phrasing implies a working intake form that doesn't exist yet.
attacks.proposed_fix: Either add an actual editable intake form (even keys/brand/state fields that adjust the benchmarkRules() multiplier), or soften step 01 copy to describe it as the demo's fixed example rather than implying user-driven intake.

attacks.attack: The site claims tariff-sensitive lines are 'flagged' during benchmarking, but no such per-line flag exists anywhere in the benchmark step — the only tariff content is one boilerplate paragraph glued onto every memo regardless of the actual bid's FF&E composition.
attacks.evidence: site/index.html step 03: "Every category is checked against sourced cost data, escalated to 2026 dollars, with tariff-sensitive lines flagged." Searching product/app.js, "tariff" appears exactly once, inside the static `renderMemo()` HTML template (line 262): a fixed bullet under "Standard documentation requests" that always fires verbatim, not tied to which categories in `results` are actually FF&E/case-goods lines. There is no tariff badge, chip, or flag rendered in `renderBenchmark()`/`bench-rows` at all.
attacks.proposed_fix: Either implement an actual per-line tariff-exposure flag (e.g., mark guestroom-softgoods/case-goods categories) or remove "tariff-sensitive lines flagged" from the how-it-works copy and describe the tariff note as a standard memo boilerplate item instead.

attacks.attack: The demand-validation protocol assumes 10-15 unknown hotel owners will grant an unbranded solo founder 20-25 minute structured calls, but the plan itself documents that this exact niche has two competitors (PIPGURU, PIPGENIUS) that have failed to generate any verifiable customer or reference after operating as waitlists — meaning owner outreach response rates for this topic are already evidenced to be weak, not just theoretically uncertain.
attacks.evidence: launch-plan.md Phase 0 Step 1 asks for 10-15 structured owner conversations recruited with no stated channel or incentive plan ("the founder ... would conduct these conversations; nothing here constitutes contacting anyone" — i.e., the recruiting mechanism itself is undesigned). Meanwhile business-plan.md / launch-plan.md Risk 3 concede PIPGURU and PIPGENIUS are "pre-revenue, waitlist-only landing pages with no named founder, no funding record, no reviews, and no independently verifiable customer" after presumably longer operating windows — evidence that owner engagement in this exact niche is hard to generate, which the plan doesn't apply back to sizing the 3-week owner-conversation recruiting effort.
attacks.proposed_fix: Add an explicit, sourced recruiting channel and expected response/conversion rate for the 10-15 calls (e.g., specific LinkedIn/AAHOA-adjacent groups, expected cold outreach-to-call conversion) before treating 3 weeks as sufficient time, and treat a failure to recruit 10-15 owners at all as its own kill signal distinct from the calls' content.

attacks.attack: The 90-day calendar compresses legally and operationally sequential work (LLC formation, trademark search, ToS/disclaimer legal review, domain registration, landing page build, AND sourcing+completing 10-15 structured owner calls) into the same first 1-3 weeks for a single solo founder, with no stated weekly time budget — making the calendar's "Week 3: assemble Phase 0 results" checkpoint optimistic on its face.
attacks.evidence: launch-plan.md 90-Day Calendar: Week 1 alone lists "Register pipbench.com; begin trademark search (TESS); begin entity formation; draft landing page with real pricing and checkout-intent button; begin sourcing 10-15 owner conversations" — five distinct workstreams with no hours-per-week estimate anywhere in launch-plan.md or business-plan.md. The plan never states the founder's available weekly hours (full-time vs. side-project), so the calendar's feasibility is unverifiable and not sourced to anything beyond the founder's own assumption.
attacks.proposed_fix: State the founder's actual available hours/week for this project explicitly in the plan, and re-sequence Week 1-3 activities against that real budget rather than listing five workstreams as if they run in a single week for one person.

attacks.attack: The FAQ's "Is PIPBench live?" answer says the demo "runs locally end to end" honestly, but doesn't disclose that it only works on one hardcoded fictional property and one hand-fitted input format — a hostile hotel-industry reader who edits the textarea with their own real PIP line-item wording will get "no benchmark category"/parse failures for most lines and could reasonably call the demo staged.
attacks.evidence: product/app.js CATEGORY_RULES (lines 15-25) match only specific keyword patterns (e.g., `/guestroom.*(soft|caseg|window|lighting)|soft goods/i`) tuned to the sample's exact wording; combined with the dot-leader parse requirement, a real owner pasting their own brand's actual PIP scope table (different wording, different formatting, no dot leaders) will see most/all lines fail to parse or fail to categorize. The site's wedge card claims this is "not a screenshot of a feature that doesn't exist yet" — technically true, but the feature only functions on inputs shaped like the demo's own fixture, undermining the implied generality.
attacks.proposed_fix: Add a second, differently-formatted sample PIP to the demo (different wording/layout) to prove the parser generalizes at least somewhat, or add an explicit caveat that the parser is tuned to common PIP scope-table phrasing and may need manual category correction on real documents.
